annotations.label: Least squares method

anchor.snippet: least squares is a method to determine the best-fit model by minimizing the sum of the squared residuals

annotations.note: Mathlib has the abstract Hilbert-space least-squares principle (orthogonal projection minimizes the residual norm) but no named statistical least-squares method.

annotations.label: Two categories of least squares problems

anchor.snippet: Least squares problems fall into two categories

annotations.note: This taxonomy (linear vs. nonlinear least squares) is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial least squares

anchor.snippet: Polynomial least squares describes the variance in a prediction of the dependent variable

annotations.note: No formalization of polynomial least squares fitting exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least squares equals maximum likelihood for exponential families

anchor.snippet: standardized least-squares estimates and maximum-likelihood estimates are identical

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks maximum-likelihood estimation and exponential families, so this equivalence is absent.

annotations.label: Residual

anchor.snippet: measured by its residual , defined as the difference between the observed value of the dependent variable

annotations.note: The statistical notion of a residual is not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: measured by its residual , defined as the difference between the observed value of the dependent variable

annotations.label: Sum of squared residuals minimization

anchor.snippet: The least-squares method finds the optimal parameter values by minimizing the sum of squared residuals

annotations.note: The orthogonal projection minimizing ‖y - x‖ over a subspace is the abstract form of minimizing the squared residual norm, generalizing the least-squares objective.

anchors.snippet: The least-squares method finds the optimal parameter values by minimizing the sum of squared residuals

annotations.label: Simplest case yields the arithmetic mean

anchor.snippet: the result of the least-squares method is the arithmetic mean of the input data

annotations.note: No lemma states that the arithmetic mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations.

annotations.label: Straight-line model

anchor.snippet: An example of a model in two dimensions is that of the straight line

annotations.note: Fitting a straight-line regression model is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient set to zero

anchor.snippet: The minimum of the sum of squares is found by setting the gradient to zero

annotations.note: Fermat's stationary-point theorem gives the general first-order necessary condition (gradient zero at a local minimum), of which setting the least-squares gradient to zero is an instance.

anchors.snippet: The minimum of the sum of squares is found by setting the gradient to zero

annotations.label: Linear regression model

anchor.snippet: A regression model is a linear one when the model comprises a linear combination of the parameters

annotations.note: Mathlib has linear maps but no formalized notion of a linear regression model.

anchors.snippet: A regression model is a linear one when the model comprises a linear combination of the parameters

annotations.label: Linear least squares closed-form solution

anchor.snippet: Setting the gradient of the loss to zero and solving for

annotations.note: The orthogonal projection provides the geometric closed-form least-squares solution, but the explicit normal-equation formula β=(XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy is not stated.

anchors.snippet: Setting the gradient of the loss to zero and solving for

annotations.label: Iterative refinement for NLLSQ

anchor.snippet: numerical algorithms are used to find the value of the parameters

annotations.note: Iterative numerical algorithms for nonlinear least squares are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: numerical algorithms are used to find the value of the parameters

annotations.label: Gauss–Newton normal equations

anchor.snippet: These are the defining equations of the Gauss–Newton algorithm

annotations.note: The Gauss–Newton algorithm and its defining equations are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear vs nonlinear in parameters

anchor.section: Differences between linear and nonlinear least squares
anchor.snippet: the model is linear in the parameters. Otherwise, the model is nonlinear

annotations.note: This classification of models by linearity in parameters is not formalized.

annotations.label: LLSQ global concavity

anchor.section: Differences between linear and nonlinear least squares
anchor.snippet: LLSQ is globally concave so non-convergence is not an issue

annotations.note: The global convexity of the linear least-squares objective is not stated as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniqueness vs multiple minima

anchor.section: Differences between linear and nonlinear least squares
anchor.snippet: In LLSQ the solution is unique, but in NLLSQ there may be multiple minima in the sum of squares

annotations.note: This comparative uniqueness statement for least-squares problems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unbiasedness of LLSQ vs NLLSQ

anchor.section: Differences between linear and nonlinear least squares
anchor.snippet: LLSQ yields unbiased estimates, but even under that condition NLLSQ estimates are generally biased

annotations.note: Estimator bias/unbiasedness in regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hooke's law spring

anchor.snippet: the extension of a spring y is proportional to the force

annotations.note: This physical least-squares example is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the extension of a spring y is proportional to the force

annotations.label: Parameter variance estimate

anchor.snippet: the variance on the j th parameter

annotations.note: Although Mathlib defines variance, the variance formula for regression parameter estimates is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the variance on the j th parameter

annotations.label: Normality of estimates under normal errors

anchor.snippet: the parameter estimates and residuals will also be normally distributed

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that linear images of a Gaussian distribution remain Gaussian, the underlying fact behind normality of linear parameter estimates, but not the regression statement itself.

annotations.label: Gauss–Markov theorem

anchor.snippet: the best linear unbiased estimator of any linear combination of the observations, is its least-squares estimator

annotations.note: The Gauss–Markov theorem (BLUE property of least squares) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least squares as maximum likelihood under normal errors

anchor.snippet: the least-squares estimators are also the maximum likelihood estimators in a linear model

annotations.note: Maximum-likelihood estimation is not formalized, so this equivalence is absent.

annotations.label: Weighted least squares

anchor.snippet: A special case of generalized least squares called weighted least squares occurs when all the off-diagonal entries

annotations.note: Weighted/generalized least squares is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heteroscedasticity

anchor.snippet: heteroscedasticity is when the variance of

annotations.note: The statistical concept of heteroscedasticity is not formalized.

annotations.label: First principal component

anchor.snippet: The first principal component about the mean of a set of points can be represented by that line which most closely approaches the data points

annotations.note: Principal component analysis and the best-fitting line characterization are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least-squares estimator as a measure

anchor.snippet: a least-squares estimator can be interpreted as a measure on the space of square-integrable functions

annotations.note: This measure-theoretic interpretation of the least-squares estimator is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tikhonov regularization

anchor.snippet: Tikhonov regularization (or ridge regression ) adds a constraint that

annotations.note: Tikhonov/ridge regularization is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lasso

anchor.snippet: An alternative regularized version of least squares is Lasso

annotations.note: The Lasso (L1-regularized) method is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lasso vs ridge regression

anchor.snippet: in ridge regression, as the penalty is increased, all parameters are reduced while still remaining non-zero

annotations.note: The comparison of Lasso and ridge shrinkage behavior is not formalized.
